perturbation.original_text: 1.1.4 “Confidential Information ” – means any and all information disclosed by Aldes, the Seller/s and/or the Business/es to the Buyer from time to time regarding, in respect of and/or related to the Business/es or Seller/s and/or for purposes of the possible acquisition o f the Business/es by the Buyer;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1.4 “Confidential Information ” – means only that information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing by Aldes, the Seller/s and/or the Business/es. This excludes any information that is generally known within the industry, or information independently developed by the Buyer.
perturbation.explanation: The modification narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The original broadly covers 'any and all information disclosed,' while the revision requires explicit marking and excludes commonly known or independently developed data. This creates conflict about what constitutes legally protected confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South Africa's Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA)
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA broadly defines personal information and mandates its protection. Limiting 'Confidential Information' to only explicitly marked data undermines the comprehensive protection intended by POPIA, as it may leave other sensitive information vulnerable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 Parliament of South Africa Long title Act to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information; to provide for the establishment of an Information Regulator to exercise certain powers and to perform certain duties and functions in terms of this Act and the Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000; to provide for the issuing of codes of conduct; to provide for the rights of persons regarding unsolicited electronic communications and automated decision making; to regulate the flow of personal information across the borders of the Republic; and to provide for matters connected therewith. Citation Act No. 4 of 2013 Territorial extent Republic of South Africa Enacted by Parliament of South Africa Assented to 19 November 2013 Commenced 1 July 2020 Keywords data protection right to privacy Status: In force South African privacy legislation This article is about South African privacy legislation. For the food, see Popiah . For the 1976 TV series, see Popi (TV series) . For the Greek singer, see Popi Malliotaki . The Protection of Personal Information Act ( PoPIA or the PoPI Act ) is a piece of legislation which governs the law of data protection and privacy in South Africa . [ 1 ] The act was passed to regulate the right to privacy, as enshrined by section 14 of the Constitution of South Africa , and would work in conjunction with the Promotion of Access to Information Act . The President of South Africa assented to the Act on 19 November 2013. As part of the regulation a new government agency was created, the Information Regulator, [ 2 ] an independent body which is empowered to monitor and enforce compliance of the PoPI Act within the public and private sector. The act came into force 1 July 2020, which commenced a one-year grace period during which all South African entities were expected to become compliant. The grace period ended 30 June 2021, with the commencement of the act on the 1 July 2021. [ 3 ] [ 4 ] [ 5 ] Core Obligations [ edit ] The PoPI Act sets out several core obligations. [ 6 ] Some of the key requirements include: Personal information can only be processed: with the consent of the data subject; or if it is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract that a data subject is a party to; or it is required by law; or it protects a legitimate interest of a data subject; or if processing is necessary for pursuing the legitimate interests of the responsible party or of a third party to whom the information is supplied. Private and public entities must report data leaks to the affected people and the Information Regulator. Organisations must appoint a responsible person who must ensure compliance to the PoPI Act. Cross-border transfers of personal data are restricted. Organis
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information,' requiring it to be explicitly marked and excluding commonly known or independently developed data. This directly contradicts the intention of POPIA, which aims for a broad protection of personal information. The Law Explanation states that limiting 'Confidential Information' undermines the comprehensive protection intended by POPIA. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the purpose of the Protection of Personal Information Act (PoPIA) is 'to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies' and 'to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information'. By narrowing the definition of confidential information, the modified contract weakens these protections, leading to non-compliance and thus, creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.2 The Buyer warrants and guarantees that it will never circumvent Aldes (albeit directly itself or indirectly through any of its subsidiaries, holding companies, group companies, related parties and/or inter -related parties or otherwise indirectly ), in negot iating or concluding any transactions , arrangements and/or agreements contemplated in clauses 2.1 and/or 2.2 above with (or with regard to) any Seller/s and/or Business /es.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.2 The Buyer warrants and guarantees that it will never circumvent Aldes, except in cases where Aldes fails to present a reasonable offer within 90 days of initial contact.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration introduces an exception to the non-circumvention clause. By allowing circumvention after 90 days if a 'reasonable offer' isn't presented, it creates ambiguity and potential conflict. What constitutes a reasonable offer is undefined, opening the door for disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Contract Law - Good Faith
perturbation.law_citation: South African Contract Law - Principles of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law emphasizes good faith. Inserting an exception that relies on a subjective 'reasonable offer' clause arguably violates the principle of good faith negotiation, as it allows the Buyer to potentially undermine Aldes' role based on a self-serving assessment.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception to the non-circumvention clause, allowing the Buyer to circumvent Aldes if a 'reasonable offer' isn't presented within 90 days. This contradicts the principle of good faith negotiation, as articulated in the Law Explanation, because 'reasonable offer' is subjective. While neither snippet directly defines good faith, the Law Explanation provides context. The modification weakens the original obligation, creating a potential for dispute and undermining the original agreement's intent, constituting a legal contradiction as it renders the obligation difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: 8.  BUYER’ S INFORMATION 8.1 The Buyer notes that Aldes’ decision to introduce any Business/es for sale to the Buyer (and the Seller/s and/or Business/es decision to enter into discussions with the Buyer) is, in part, based on the information presented by the Buyer under and in terms of the Buyer’s Profile on the face of this Agreement. As such, the Buyer warrants and guarantees that all information presented and p rovided via and in terms of the Buyer’s Profile was, is and will remain true, complete and accurate and that it will immediately inform Aldes should there be any change in t he information provided/presented.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.  BUYER’ S INFORMATION 8.1 The Buyer notes that Aldes’ decision to introduce any Business/es for sale to the Buyer (and the Seller/s and/or Business/es decision to enter into discussions with the Buyer) is based on the information presented by the Buyer. The Buyer warrants that the provided information is accurate to the best of their current knowledge, but disclaims any liability for unintentional errors or omissions.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'disclaims any liability for unintentional errors or omissions,' the modified text weakens the original warranty of truthfulness. The original agreement requires complete accuracy, while the alteration excuses unintentional inaccuracies, creating uncertainty about the Buyer's responsibility for the information provided.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Law of Contract - Misrepresentation
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law holds parties accountable for misrepresentations. While unintentional misrepresentations may have different remedies than fraudulent ones, disclaiming all liability undermines the principle that parties must ensure the accuracy of information they provide during contract formation. The disclaimer could potentially shield the Buyer even from negligent misrepresentation, conflicting with established legal principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Analysis Of The ILPA Model Fund Agreement - Financial Services - United States ARTICLE 22 May 2020 Analysis Of The ILPA Model Fund Agreement MB Mayer Brown More Contributor Mayer Brown is a distinctively global law firm, uniquely positioned to advise the world’s leading companies and financial institutions on their most complex deals and disputes. We have deep experience in high-stakes litigation and complex transactions across industry sectors, including our signature strength, the global financial services industry. Explore Firm Details On October 30, 2019, the Institutional Limited Partners Association ("ILPA") publicly released a model Limited Partnership Agreement for private equity buyout funds. United States Finance and Banking Todd N. Bundrant, Mark Dempsey, Iliana Kirova +8 Authors Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. On October 30, 2019, the Institutional Limited Partners Association ("ILPA") publicly released a model Limited Partnership Agreement for private equity buyout funds. The Model LPA is intended to represent the ILPA Principles 3.0, which offer a series of benchmark provisions intended by ILPA to promote a closer alignment of interests among sponsors and investors. In light of ILPA's stature and the influence of earlier versions of the Principles on the private fund industry, we expect that some of the Model LPA terms will be raised in the course of negotiations, particularly those involving first-time fund sponsors. As highlighted in this Legal Update, the Model LPA generally reflects negotiated positions that are more favorable to investors than to sponsors. Our analysis is intended to help facilitate review of ILPA's proposed benchmarks for all parties as we move into a more competitive fundraising environment. Downloads - Download Document Visit us at mayerbrown.com Mayer Brown is a global legal services provider comprising legal practices that are separate entities (the "Mayer Brown Practices"). The Mayer Brown Practices are: Mayer Brown LLP and Mayer Brown Europe - Brussels LLP, both limited liability partnerships established in Illinois USA; Mayer Brown International LLP, a limited liability partnership incorporated in England and Wales (authorized and regulated by the Solicitors Regulation Authority and registered in England and Wales number OC 303359); Mayer Brown, a SELAS established in France; Mayer Brown JSM, a Hong Kong partnership and its associated entities in Asia; and Tauil & Chequer Advogados, a Brazilian law partnership with which Mayer Brown is associated. "Mayer Brown" and the Mayer Brown logo are the trademarks of the Mayer Brown Practices in their respective jurisdictions. © Copyright 2020. The Mayer Brown Practices. All rights reserved. The foregoing is not a comprehensive treatment of the subject matter covered and is not intended to provide legal advice. Readers should seek specific legal advice before taking any action with respect to the matters discussed herein. perturbation.justification: The modification in the 'Changed Text' introduces a disclaimer ('disclaims any liability for unintentional errors or omissions') that weakens the original warranty of truthfulness. The 'Law Explanation' suggests this undermines the principle that parties must ensure the accuracy of information they provide during contract formation and conflicts with established legal principles concerning misrepresentation. The original text requires the buyer to guarantee that all provided information is true, complete, and accurate, while the changed text allows for unintentional inaccuracies without liability. This is a legal contradiction because the misaligned terminology directly contradicts the original agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1.1 “Aldes ” – means and includes Aldes Business Brokers Franchise Africa (Pty) Ltd (Registration Number: 2006/036900/07) and any and all of its subsidiaries, any and all of its holding companies, any other entities forming part of its group of companies and/or any and all of its franchisees from time to time, or any one of them as the context may require;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1.1 “Aldes ” – means Aldes Business Brokers Franchise Africa (Pty) Ltd (Registration Number: 2006/036900/07) only. It does not include its subsidiaries, holding companies, group companies, or franchisees.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the definition of 'Aldes' to only the named company and excluding its subsidiaries, holding companies, group companies, and franchisees, the modification significantly reduces the scope of the agreement. This contradicts the original intent of including the entire Aldes network.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Companies Act, Section 1 (Definition of 'Subsidiary')
perturbation.law_explanation: The South African Companies Act provides a legal definition of 'subsidiary'. By explicitly excluding subsidiaries from the definition of 'Aldes,' the modified contract language may create legal issues. For example, if a subsidiary of Aldes disclosed confidential information, it is unclear whether the contract's confidentiality provisions would apply, directly conflicting with the broad coverage that typically exists due to the definition of subsidiary under the Act.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text includes subsidiaries, holding companies, group companies and franchisees in its definition of 'Aldes.' The changed text explicitly excludes these entities. The Law Explanation suggests that this exclusion could create legal issues, specifically regarding confidentiality provisions, by limiting the application of the contract. The Perturbation Explanation also states the change contradicts the original intent by reducing the scope of the agreement. Even though the scraped law snippets are unhelpful, the legal explanation and the perturbation explanation both identify a legal contradiction based on the potential implications of excluding the subsidiaries from the contract, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 5. LIABILITY 5.1 The Buyer acknowledges that it will be liable , responsible and accountable to each of Aldes , the Business/es and the Seller/s (jointly and severally) for a breach of this Agreement by itself or any of its related or inter related parties, employees, consultants, ad visers and/or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. LIABILITY 5.1 The Buyer acknowledges that it will be liable, responsible, and accountable only for a breach of this Agreement directly caused by its own actions. It will not be held responsible for breaches by its related parties, employees, consultants, advisers, and/or agents unless gross negligence on the part of the buyer can be proven.
perturbation.explanation: This shifts the liability. The original clause makes the Buyer liable for breaches by related parties, employees, etc. The modified version requires proof of gross negligence, adding a significant burden of proof. This contradiction affects the enforceability of the liability clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Law of Agency - Vicarious Liability
perturbation.law_citation: Vicarious Liability under South African Law of Agency
perturbation.law_explanation: South African law recognizes vicarious liability, where a principal can be liable for the actions of their agent. By requiring proof of gross negligence, the modified clause attempts to limit the Buyer's vicarious liability, potentially contradicting established legal principles. It may be argued the Buyer could be held vicariously liable in many circumstances. This modification weakens the protection that the original clause provided to the seller/s and Business/es.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text holds the buyer liable for breaches by related parties, employees, etc. The changed text modifies this by requiring proof of gross negligence on the part of the buyer to hold them responsible for breaches by those related parties. The Law Explanation states this attempts to limit the Buyer's vicarious liability, potentially contradicting established legal principles, and weakens the protection that the original clause provided to the seller/s and Business/es. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction', where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. One LexisNexis snippet states they can provide access to critical research, which would likely be needed when determining the vicarious liability, a task made more difficult given the changed terms.
